questions.stem: An 80-year-old man presents with syncope. On examination, heart rate is 35 bpm, BP 80/40 mmHg. There are cannon a waves and variable intensity of S1. ECG shows P waves at 90 bpm and QRS complexes at 35 bpm, with no relationship between them. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Atrial fibrillation with slow ventricular response
questions.explanation: Complete AV block is characterized by complete dissociation of atrial and ventricular activity, resulting in an escape rhythm. The presence of cannon a waves and variable S1 intensity are classic signs. Sinus bradycardia would have a 1:1 relationship, and atrial fibrillation would show no P waves.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with an acute inferior STEMI develops bradycardia (50 bpm). ECG shows progressive lengthening of the PR interval over several beats, followed by a dropped QRS complex. The PR interval then resets. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The pattern of progressive PR prolongation with then a dropped beat is pathognomonic for Mobitz type I AV block, often seen in inferior MI due to heightened vagal tone or nodal ischaemia. Mobitz type II has fixed PR interval, and 2:1 AV block does not show the Wenckebach pattern.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman presents with syncope. She has no chest pain. ECG shows 2:1 AV block, with a constant PR interval (0.20 s) and a wide QRS (0.14 s). A longer rhythm strip does not show any other patterns. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Atrial flutter with fixed AV block
Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) AV block with 2:1 conduction
questions.explanation: 2:1 AV block cannot be classified by pattern alone, but a normal PR interval and wide QRS suggest infranodal block typical of Mobitz type II. Mobitz type I usually has PR prolongation and narrow QRS. Complete AV block would show complete dissociation, not just every second P wave blocked.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with palpitations and lightheadedness. He recently returned from a camping trip and has a large erythematous rash with central clearing on his thigh. ECG shows PR interval of 320 ms, and every P wave is conducted to the ventricle. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Lyme disease (erythema migrans) frequently causes first-degree AV block. The prolonged PR interval with 1:1 conduction is diagnostic, and Mobitz I would have dropped beats, while complete AV block would have dissociation. Sick sinus syndrome involves sinus node dysfunction, not PR prolongation.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman with a history of anterior MI presents with collapse. ECG shows atrial rate of 90 bpm and ventricular rate of 35 bpm. P waves are seen to march through the QRS complexes with no relationship. The QRS complexes are wide (0.16 s) and regular. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Complete AV block is diagnosed when no P waves conduct to the ventricles; here there is complete dissociation with a broad escape rhythm from the Purkinje system. High-grade AV block would have some conducted beats. Mobitz type II would show some conducted P waves with fixed PR. Ventricular tachycardia is unlikely with rate 35.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with hypertension and diabetes attends for a routine health check. He is asymptomatic. His ECG shows a PR interval of 240 ms, with narrow QRS complexes and no dropped beats. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Second-degree AV block Mobitz type I
Second-degree AV block Mobitz type II
questions.explanation: A PR interval >200 ms with consistent conduction defines first-degree AV block. The absence of dropped beats or progressive PR lengthening excludes Mobitz I/II and third-degree block.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman presents with palpitations and occasional dizziness. Her ECG shows a regular rhythm with progressive PR prolongation culminating in a non-conducted P wave. The QRS complexes are narrow. What is the diagnosis?
options: Second-degree AV block Mobitz type II
Second-degree AV block Mobitz type I (Wenckebach)
questions.explanation: Progressive PR prolongation followed by a dropped QRS is classic for Mobitz I. The narrow QRS suggests the block is at the AV node level.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man presents with syncope. His ECG shows sinus rhythm at 80 bpm with a constant PR interval of 180 ms. Suddenly, a P wave is not followed by a QRS complex. This pattern repeats every few beats. The QRS complexes are broad. What is the diagnosis?
options: Second-degree AV block Mobitz type II
Second-degree AV block Mobitz type I
Atrial fibrillation with slow ventricular response
questions.explanation: Constant PR with intermittent non-conducted P waves is characteristic of Mobitz II. Broad QRS suggests infranodal block, often requiring pacing.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman presents with breathlessness and syncope. Her pulse is 35 bpm. ECG shows P waves at a rate of 80/min that are completely dissociated from the QRS complexes. The ventricular rate is 35 bpm. What is the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Complete atrioventricular dissociation with an atrial rate faster than the ventricular rate is diagnostic of third-degree AV block. The slow ventricular escape rhythm confirms block.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man reports episodic lightheadedness lasting seconds to minutes. His resting 12-lead ECG is normal. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm intermittent AV block?
questions.explanation: A 24-hour Holter monitor is the standard test to capture intermittent arrhythmias like AV block. Exercise ECG can provoke ischaemia but not reliably induce block; echo and MRI assess structure, not rhythm; tilt test for vasovagal syncope.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with central chest pain and ST elevation in leads II, III, aVF. ECG shows complete heart block with a ventricular rate of 40 bpm. BP 100/70 mmHg. He is alert and pain relieved. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: In inferior MI with AV block, atropine is first-line for haemodynamically stable patients. Transcutaneous pacing is reserved for those unstable or unresponsive to atropine. Isoprenaline is an alternative but not first-line. Permanent pacing is not indicated acutely.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old asymptomatic marathon runner has a routine ECG showing first-degree AV block (PR interval 240 ms). He has no symptoms, normal BP, and normal exercise tolerance. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.explanation: First-degree AV block is often benign and requires no intervention in asymptomatic individuals, especially in athletes. Atropine or beta-blockers are not indicated, and pacemaker is reserved for symptomatic bradycardia or advanced block.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman on digoxin for heart failure presents with nausea and dizziness. ECG shows second-degree AV block Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) with a heart rate of 55 bpm. Serum digoxin level is 3.2 ng/mL. She is haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: In stable digoxin toxicity causing type I second-degree AV block, the first step is to stop the drug. Digoxin-specific Fab is reserved for life-threatening toxicity (e.g., severe bradyarrhythmia, ventricular arrhythmia). Atropine and pacing are second-line if symptoms worsen.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man presents with syncope. ECG shows complete heart block with ventricular rate of 35 bpm. BP 80/50 mmHg. He is dizzy and clammy. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: In haemodynamically unstable complete heart block, immediate transcutaneous pacing is the first-line emergency treatment. Atropine is unlikely to be effective in complete heart block (especially if infranodal). Transvenous pacing is definitive but takes longer; transcutaneous provides immediate support.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with known 2:1 AV block (Mobitz type II) and left bundle branch block presents with atrial fibrillation and a ventricular rate of 140 bpm. He is haemodynamically stable. Which of the following drugs is contraindicated in his management?
questions.explanation: Verapamil is absolutely contraindicated in patients with second- or third-degree AV block as it further depresses AV conduction. Beta-blockers (metoprolol) and digoxin are relatively contraindicated but can be used with caution. Amiodarone and flecainide are safer in terms of AV conduction but flecainide is not first-line for rate control.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman has a routine ECG for life insurance. It shows sinus rhythm with a PR interval of 240 ms. She is asymptomatic and has no medical history. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start oral atenolol 50 mg once daily
questions.explanation: First-degree AV block in an asymptomatic patient with no underlying heart disease is a benign finding. NICE guidelines do not recommend referral or treatment; reassurance is appropriate.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with hypertension presents to the Emergency Department after a syncopal episode. An ECG shows 2:1 AV block with a narrow QRS and PR interval is constant on the conducted beats. His blood pressure is 128/76 mmHg and heart rate is 45 bpm. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Urgent referral for permanent pacemaker insertion
questions.explanation: This is Mobitz type II second-degree AV block, which carries a high risk of progression to complete heart block. Urgent permanent pacemaker insertion is indicated. Atropine is unlikely to be effective and may worsen block.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man is admitted with acute inferior ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction. He develops complete heart block with a wide QRS escape rhythm at 35 bpm. His blood pressure is 70/40 mmHg and he is diaphoretic. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: In unstable bradycardia due to complete heart block, immediate temporary pacing is the definitive treatment. Atropine is less effective in high-degree AV block, and while revascularisation is important, pacing is the most urgent next step.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman with a history of hypertension presents with recurrent syncope. Her ECG shows complete heart block with a narrow QRS escape rhythm at 40 bpm. Her blood pressure is 118/72 mmHg and she is alert and comfortable. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Admit for elective permanent pacemaker insertion
questions.explanation: Stable complete heart block with narrow QRS escape is likely due to AV nodal block and requires permanent pacing. Admission is needed for pacemaker insertion; temporary pacing is reserved for unstable patients. Atropine is ineffective.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old asymptomatic swimmer presents for pre-participation screening. ECG shows sinus rhythm with PR interval 240 ms. Which additional ECG finding would most strongly indicate a pathological rather than benign first-degree AV block?
options: QRS duration 140 ms with left bundle branch block pattern
T wave inversion in leads V1-V3
questions.explanation: Isolated first-degree AV block in a young athlete is usually benign. However, associated bifascicular block (e.g., left bundle branch block) suggests underlying conduction system disease and risk of progression to high-grade block.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with diabetes presents with exertional dizziness. ECG shows Mobitz II second-degree AV block (intermittent dropped beats with constant PR interval). Which clinical finding from history or examination is most concerning for impending complete heart block?
questions.explanation: Syncope in the setting of Mobitz II implies transient complete heart block with cerebral hypoperfusion, a critical red flag that necessitates urgent pacing. Other symptoms are less specific for imminent progression.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman with hypertension is brought to the ED after a fall. She is confused, HR 32 bpm, BP 90/60. ECG shows complete heart block with a wide QRS escape rhythm. Which ECG feature best identifies that the escape rhythm is of ventricular origin and carries a higher risk of asystole?
options: T wave inversion in the precordial leads
questions.explanation: A wide QRS (>120 ms) escape rhythm indicates an idioventricular pacemaker distal to the bifurcation, which is less reliable and slower than a junctional escape. This finding heightens the need for urgent pacing, as the risk of asystole is increased.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with atrial fibrillation on digoxin presents with nausea and bradycardia (HR 45 bpm). ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a regular, narrow-complex rhythm. Which finding is most suggestive of digoxin toxicity causing complete heart block with junctional escape?
options: T wave inversion in the lateral leads
Regularization of the previously irregular ventricular response
ST segment depression in leads V5-V6
questions.explanation: Regularization of the ventricular rhythm in a patient with atrial fibrillation indicates complete AV block with a junctional escape rhythm, a classic sign of digoxin toxicity. This is a dangerous finding that warrants immediate digoxin level check and holding the drug.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with recurrent syncope has an ECG showing sinus rhythm with left bundle branch block. Which finding on 24-hour Holter monitoring would most strongly implicate intermittent complete heart block as the cause of syncope?
options: Episodes of Mobitz I second-degree AV block
Episodes of Mobitz II second-degree AV block
questions.explanation: Mobitz II in a patient with bifascicular block (left bundle branch block counts as one fascicle, but actually LBBB is a single fascicle; however, in context, left bundle branch block plus syncope suggests possible trifascicular disease. The finding of Mobitz II indicates infranodal block and a high risk of progression to complete heart block, which is a treatable cause of syncope.
